artifacts.content:
Hi Angie,

It’s almost midnight and I’m too wired to sleep again, so I figured I’d write instead of scrolling. Ethan finally crashed after finishing that Colorado history project – he’s obsessed with the mining disasters now, of all things. I packed his lunch for tomorrow and set my 6:15 alarm like I still have to be at FrontRange by 8, even though they’ve had me on unpaid leave since 11/1. Muscle memory, I guess.

I keep thinking about how all these “contacts” in my life have shifted. Legally, Luis is still the first person they’re supposed to call if something happens to me – that temporary custody order from Denver County Family Court back in March 2023 isn’t going anywhere. After I ended up at Denver Health last October for that anxiety episode, every hospital form I touched I just wrote his name automatically.

Then there’s you, my unofficial CFO. Since they yanked my paychecks, you’ve been the one helping juggle the late Xcel bill and reminding me which card is least on fire. I still feel guilty that you flew up in early May 2024 and stayed five days in my little W 32nd Ave apartment just because I admitted I was barely sleeping and drinking two or three glasses of wine every night after they put me on leave.

Honestly, at this point I don’t even care about “winning.” Part of me just wants them to have to face what they did – like, actually feel some of the humiliation and stress they dumped on me, even if that meant standing in front of my old team and saying, “We screwed up.”

Anyway, enough ranting. How are your kiddos at the center? Send me a picture of your classroom art wall if you think of it.

Love you,
Maria

Mami,

I’m sitting here with my blood pressure cuff on my arm (again) and wanted to update you before I forget half of it. I talked to that legal aid lady this afternoon about what I’m hoping comes out of all this mess with FrontRange. I told her the money isn’t just about “pain and suffering” or whatever – it’s about very real bills.

If I could get enough to wipe out the $18,400 from the Denver Health Medical Center ER visit on 12/19/25 and all the follow-up tests, plus what I’ve been paying Dr. Karen Liu at Riverside Behavioral Health – $220 every other Tuesday since January – I feel like I could finally breathe. Right now I keep putting her sessions on my credit card and pretending that’s sustainable.

On top of that, I’m dreaming of something around $65,000. That sounds huge until I do the math: a year of rent and groceries, catching up the $9,700 Luis still owes in back child support from when he lost his auto body job in April 2025, and keeping Ethan in the St. Mary’s Learning Center summer program. You know how much he loves it there, and $285 a week adds up so fast I get nauseous just thinking about it.

Honestly, if they just offered a decent lump sum so I could walk away and never have to hear “FrontRange Health Services” again, I’d probably take it and be done. I’m tired of reliving every awful conversation.

Anyway, how was dialysis today? Did Dad remember to pack your sweater for the clinic? I’ll call you tomorrow after Ethan’s soccer practice.

Te quiero,
Maria

Journal – can’t sleep again

I keep replaying the stuff Kevin and Danielle actually saw, like I’m trying to convince myself I didn’t imagine it.

9/2/25 – Sitting in Danielle O’Connor’s little HR office at the FrontRange main campus, shaking so hard I could barely hold the cup of water. I told her how Jennifer’s last‑minute schedule changes were making me miss cardiology follow‑ups at Denver Health Medical Center. I remember blurting out that I’d already had to pay a $75 no‑show fee for the August appointment she made me miss by changing my shift the night before. Danielle watched me cry and said she understood why I was scared about my heart.

Then 9/16/25 – back in Danielle’s office in the HR suite. She said a coworker had seen my anxiety meds bottle on my desk during a late August evening shift and overheard Jennifer saying, “If Maria can’t handle the stress without pills, maybe this isn’t the right place for her.” I wanted to disappear. I only started those meds after the doctor at the community clinic on Federal Boulevard told me the panic attacks and blood pressure spikes were getting worse because of work.

When I think about everything, part of me just wants to say yes to whatever halfway‑reasonable thing they throw at me. I’m so worn down that even a small gesture feels tempting, just so I can stop dragging all this around.

Tomorrow: call Riverside Behavioral to confirm next Tuesday with Dr. Liu, and set a reminder to check Ethan’s inhaler refills before soccer.

Hey Kevin,

Just wanted to say thanks again for talking me down on the phone earlier. I’m still buzzing a little, so I’m writing this out to get it out of my head before I have to be up at 6:15 to wrangle Ethan and get him to Edison by 7:50.

I keep going back to that stretch last fall. On 9/9/25, by the 4th floor break room at FrontRange, when I asked Jennifer for a small schedule tweak so I could get to the neurology follow‑up at Denver Health for my migraines and insomnia, she said, right in front of you, “Between your migraines, the anxiety meds you’ve been on since Ethan’s custody hearing last March, and all those appointments, I don’t know how reliable you can be for full‑time work anymore at your age.” You told me after the 3:00 p.m. huddle that you heard her drag my meds and family court stuff into it and you felt gross.

Then 9/12/25 in Conference Room B – you were at your desk like 10–15 feet away. You heard her raise her voice and throw that $600 paycheck advance from April back in my face: that I should be “grateful” they didn’t fire me over being overdrawn when I was behind on rent at 2874 W 32nd. And the whole “if you keep making waves with HR you won’t like the consequences” thing.

Meanwhile, real life keeps going. Ethan’s science fair form is due Friday, Mom’s dialysis supplies are taking over their Peoria apartment, and I still need to figure out what to make for dinner that isn’t frozen pizza.

Thanks for being in my corner.

– Maria

metadata.subject: Trying to make sense of it

artifacts.content:
Planning – money + housing

I need to be brutally honest about what I’m trying to protect here.

1) This apartment: 2874 W 32nd Ave, Apt 3B. I just signed that 12‑month renewal before Danielle called me into the little HR conference room next to her office on 11/1/25 to say I was being put on unpaid administrative leave. I remember telling her right there that my rent is $1,640 a month and that I’m already two weeks behind on the September credit card payment I used for Ethan’s asthma inhalers and school clothes. She saw me spiraling about how to keep up with everything.

2) Not having to move in with Mom and Dad on South Peoria in Aurora. With Mom’s kidney failure diagnosis in March 2024 and all the home dialysis equipment taking over the second bedroom, Dad’s already sleeping on the couch. There really isn’t space for Ethan and me, and the idea of uprooting him from Highland and Edison Elementary makes my stomach hurt.

3) Enough front pay – at least nine months – so I can stay put while I job hunt and keep Ethan stable. I wrote it out earlier: nine months of rent, groceries, his after‑school program at Lake Middle School once he starts there, and the bus pass.

Action items tomorrow: update the color‑coded job spreadsheet, call Mom to see if she still wants me at Sunday dinner, and set up a budget check‑in with Angela (she’s been covering $450 of my rent since mid‑August 2024).

metadata.title: What I need to feel safe

artifacts.content:
Event: Unemployment Appeal Hearing – CO Dept. of Labor
Date: Saturday, 2026-04-18
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 11:00 a.m.
Location: Colorado Department of Labor, 18th Street office, Denver
Attendees: Me

Notes:

• Bring folder with timeline (final written warning in January 2024, panic attack at FrontRange 4th floor office on 3/18/24 when Kevin had to drive me to Denver Health Medical Center, unpaid administrative leave starting 5/6/24 at the downtown office, then again 11/1/25).

• Their written response says I was terminated for “dishonesty” over a supposedly falsified timesheet from the week of 2025-09-08. Need to be clear that my log‑ins matched the phone system and that no one mentioned a problem with that week until after I started complaining about Jennifer’s comments and the schedule games.

• Goal: secure ongoing unemployment so I can keep paying rent at 2874 W 32nd and Ethan’s after‑school program fees at Lake.

• Remind myself: Luis has been doing the Wednesday exchanges since the May 2023 custody modification, and he’s seen how wiped out I am after shifts and hearings. If needed, he can write a short statement later about how this has affected Ethan.

To‑do before hearing:
– Lay out clothes night before so I’m not scrambling with Ethan’s soccer gear that morning.
– Ask Mom if she can watch him or if Luis can take that Saturday.
– Go to bed by 10 p.m. the night before (no doom‑scrolling).

Angie,

I just got off the phone with you but of course now my brain is spinning again, so here’s the long version you didn’t ask for.

I keep thinking about how many people have had to step in for me since all this started. After that panic attack at the FrontRange call center on 4/18/24, Mom was the one who picked me up from the ER at Saint Joseph Hospital and then stayed three nights with me at the apartment, making sure I actually ate and didn’t just lie there staring at the ceiling.

You’ve basically become my backup everything – handling my bills from Albuquerque, flying up in early May 2024 to stay five days when I admitted I was drinking wine every night and not sleeping, and now being my “in case of emergency” person when I list contacts anywhere. Meanwhile, Dad quietly keeps the Aurora storage unit key on his ring because he’s the one who helped me move half my furniture there after I sold the car for $3,500 last August to cover that back child support mess after the Denver County courthouse review hearing.

On the work side, Danielle O’Connor has been this weird mix of helpful and frustrating. She met with me three times between March and June 2024 in that 5th floor HR conference room about the hostile comments, and then she’s also the one who told me on 11/1/25 I was going on unpaid leave. I still don’t know how much she actually pushed back.

Anyway, I just wanted you to know I see how much you’re carrying with me. I’ll send you Ethan’s new soccer schedule tomorrow – we finally got the email.

Besos,
Maria

metadata.subject: You, Mom, Dad, and all the pieces you’re holding

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Thompson <m.thompson84@gmail.com>
To: Danielle Marie O’Connor <[redacted]>
Date: 2025-11-18
Subject: Follow-up details for my internal complaint notes

Hi Danielle,

I’m pulling everything together like you suggested after our 2025-09-02 meeting, and I wanted to summarize who’s involved and what they’ve witnessed so it’s clear if this goes further.

For professional matters related to this complaint, my main contact person is my close friend and former team lead, Kevin Douglas Harper. At the employer FrontRange Health Services downtown Denver office, he’s been advising me informally since my final written warning in January 2024, through the performance improvement plan, the three-day suspension my supervisor gave me at the FrontRange Health Services office, and then my placement on unpaid administrative leave on May 6, 2024. He also knows how things escalated after my internal HR complaint. On 2025-10-07, in Conference Room A at the FrontRange Health Services Denver office, he heard Jennifer Collins say during a holiday coverage strategy meeting, “We should plan the holiday coverage without counting on Maria… she’s probably not going to be around much longer,” while referencing my migraines and the FMLA paperwork I filed after my dad Javier Luis Martinez’s heart procedure in Aurora in August 2025.

In our initial meeting on 2025-09-02, you also witnessed me telling you that my ex-husband, Luis Alejandro Thompson, had threatened during a phone call the night before to go back to Jefferson County court if my work stress and schedule changes at the FrontRange Health Services downtown call center kept making me late to Ethan’s 5:30 p.m. pickups at his after-school program. I told you Jennifer’s last-minute shift extensions had already caused me to miss pick-up twice in August 2025, and you saw how terrified I was that Luis might use that against me in another custody modification.

Thanks again for documenting all of this,
Maria

metadata.subject: Follow-up details for my internal complaint notes

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Thompson <m.thompson84@gmail.com>
To: Rosa Elena Martinez <[redacted]>
Cc: Angela Renee Martinez <[redacted]>
Date: 2025-12-03
Subject: Witness list + what I’m asking for

Mami and Angie,

I finally sat down to draft what they’re calling my “settlement position” and the list of witnesses.

Witness 1 is Luis Alejandro Thompson – my ex-husband. Our divorce was finalized in July 2020 in Denver County Court, and we’ve been co-parenting Ethan Miguel Thompson since then. He’s supervised our son’s court-ordered Wednesday evening exchanges with me at the FrontRange Health Services parking lot ever since the custody modification hearing in May 2023. He’s also the one who picked me up outside the FrontRange Health Services parking garage on March 29, 2024, after I called him sobbing because my manager had just told me I was being put on unpaid administrative leave (no income from the employer). He saw my condition that night and can talk about how the work situation has affected Ethan.

Witness 2 is you, Mami – Rosa Elena Martinez. You’ve been helping me cover $450 of my monthly rent at 2874 W 32nd Avenue since my paychecks stopped in mid-August 2024, and you were with me in the Aurora clinic waiting room right after the incident when I broke down crying. You’ve also been the one taking Ethan to his recurring biweekly counseling appointments at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora, CO since February 2024, because he started having anxiety about me possibly losing my job at FrontRange Health Services.

As for what I would accept to settle this complaint: I’m asking for reimbursement for the three missed cardiology appointments at the UCHealth University of Colorado Hospital cardiology clinic in Aurora, CO between 2025-10-15 and 2025-11-30. I canceled those high blood pressure and chest pain workup visits because my supervisor, Kevin Douglas Harper, implied my job would be at risk if I kept taking time off.

Love,
Maria

metadata.subject: Witness list + what I’m asking for

artifacts.content:
Google Keep Note – Draft for Complaint Form
Last edited: 2025-12-05
Title: Contacts, witnesses, and incident context

Contact person for professional matters:
For anything related to my complaint against FrontRange Health Services, my contact person is my close friend and former team lead Kevin Douglas Harper. He’s been advising me informally about the complaint since my final written warning in January 2024 and my placement on unpaid administrative leave on May 6, 2024 at the FrontRange Health Services downtown Denver office. Since the start of my unpaid administrative leave on August 14, 2024, I haven’t been able to afford my full rent, and Kevin has been letting me stay in his spare bedroom in Lakewood.

Witness 1 options:
• Luis Alejandro Thompson – ex-husband, co-parent of our son, Ethan Miguel Thompson. He and I have been co-parenting since our divorce was finalized in July 2020 in Denver County Court. He picked me up outside the FrontRange Health Services parking garage on March 29, 2024, right after I was told I was being put on unpaid administrative leave, and he’s supervised our court-ordered Wednesday exchanges at the FrontRange Health Services parking lot since the May 2023 custody modification hearing.
• Mom, Rosa Elena Martinez – retired medical assistant. She’s been taking Ethan to his counseling appointments at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora, CO since February 2024 for his anxiety about me possibly losing my job at FrontRange Health Services.

Witness 2 options:
• Younger sister, Angela Renee Martinez – has been helping me manage panic attacks and insomnia since February 2024 and was on the phone with me outside the FrontRange Health Services building the night of the incident.
• Mom (again) – has been covering $450 of my monthly rent at 2874 W 32nd Avenue since my paychecks stopped in mid-August 2024 and sat with me in the Aurora clinic waiting room after the incident when I broke down crying.

Reminder: emphasize that Kevin, as close friend and former team lead, is both my main professional contact and Witness 2, because he’s seen the impact of the unpaid leave and housing instability firsthand.

From: Maria Thompson <mjt.denver84@gmail.com>
To: Maria Thompson <mjt.denver84+notes@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-12-03 21:17
Subject: Notes from call w/ Danielle O. re: FRHS situation

Saving this so I don’t lose track of the timeline.

Danielle Marie O’Connor – professional HR acquaintance – really talked me down tonight. I reminded her that we first met at that HR networking breakfast at the Sheraton Denver Downtown Hotel back in October 2022, when Kevin dragged me along because he said I needed to “see how the other side thinks.” I honestly never expected that casual Sheraton breakfast to turn into my main lifeline two years later.

Since March 2024, Danielle has been informally advising me by phone about how to handle the performance improvement plan my unnamed supervisor at FrontRange Health Services put me on. She said again tonight that the way the PIP was rolled out felt “retaliatory and poorly documented,” especially given my prior performance reviews.

We went over the three-day suspension my supervisor gave me right before putting me on unpaid leave. Danielle’s view: the three-day suspension prior to unpaid leave looks like they were building a paper trail rather than actually coaching me. She told me to keep every email, calendar invite, and Teams chat from the FrontRange Health Services office, especially anything mentioning my medical appointments or schedule changes.

Action items from the call:
- Draft a written summary of my concerns and send it to HR so there’s a clear record.
- Ask for copies of all performance evaluations prior to the performance improvement plan.
- Keep logging dates/times of any calls with HR or my supervisor.

She also reminded me to protect my mental health and stick with therapy, even while all of this drags on.

metadata.subject: Notes from call w/ Danielle O. re: FRHS situation
